explicit_text: I was in school on Pearl Harbor Day, December 7th, 1941. At that time, I was only 17 years old and thought the war wouldn't last long. It was a significant event that eventually led to me receiving my draft notice when the draft age was lowered to 18.

explicit_text: I was born in Bridgeville, Delaware, on September 7th, 1924. It’s where I had my first beer at a fireman's parade many years ago, probably in the early '60s. My family roots run deep there, as I come from the Adams family, known as the scrapple boys down in that area.

explicit_text: In 1925, my family moved from Bridgeville to Canterbury, Delaware, where I lived for about three years with my grandmother's cousin. Later, my mother bought a little farm on the Canterbury Milford Road, and I was raised there on that farm. It was a small town but home to me during my childhood.

explicit_text: I was drafted into the United States Army on October 8th, 1943, after turning 18 and losing my deferment from farming. I trained at Camp Croft in South Carolina and was eventually sent overseas as a replacement soldier. Serving in the Army during World War II shaped much of my early adult life.

explicit_text: After some rest following the Rome campaign, we went down to Salerno, south of Naples, for amphibious training. This was in preparation for the invasion of southern France. Salerno was an important training and staging area for that operation in mid-1944.
